value:
Dr. Ilana Wisby is a deep tech entrepreneur and advisor, known for her pioneering leadership in quantum computing and emotionally intelligent organisations. As founding CEO of Oxford Quantum Circuits (OQC), she helped shape Europe’s quantum landscape, championed love-based leadership, and is now building a new venture in stealth.
Why did you choose to be a physicist, and in particular what has attracted you to the field of quantum science and technology?
I’ve always been deeply curious about how the world works - both on a human level and a physical one. My mum, a social worker, instilled in me a fascination with people and behaviour, while my dad, an engineer, encouraged hands-on exploration that sparked my love for how things function. That questioning instinct naturally led me to physics. I fell in love with quantum physics, and particularly the creativity of experimental work when I experienced how the elegance of mathematics could be mapped onto reality. Watching abstract equations take shape in the lab was both intellectually rigorous and emotionally grounding. I’ve worked in other areas of deep tech, including AI, but once you’ve hit the edge of quantum, it’s hard to look away! The field continues to inspire me because it invites deep thinking, creative problem-solving, and the chance to contribute to technologies with the potential to make a profoundly positive impact on humanity.
What scientific or technological development(s) are you most excited about?
We are finally seeing quantum technology make a meaningful difference in the real world - particularly in drug discovery and clean energy. Hybrid quantum-classical models are simulating molecular interactions with precision once thought unreachable, while quantum algorithms are informing the design of catalysts and battery materials. These aren’t hypothetical futures - they’re being deployed to solve real, urgent global problems.
At the same time, fields like quantum networking and quantum biology are pushing the boundaries of what we consider computable or even knowable. I’m equally excited by how new technologies are reshaping the process of innovation itself. With increasingly powerful and accessible tools, accelerated by AI, discovery is becoming more distributed, iterative, and creative. We’re entering a new era - where science not only moves faster, but with greater intention. And I believe we’re nearing the next great shift in how we understand life, intelligence, and the nature of reality.
In your view, what is the greatest challenge or open problem in quantum science and technology and how can it be overcome?
The greatest challenge in quantum science today is building systems that are both scalable and fault-tolerant. We can now control small numbers of qubits with impressive precision - but scaling to the millions of high-fidelity qubits needed for error-corrected, general-purpose quantum computing remains a significant hurdle. It’s not just about more qubits; it’s about stability, manufacturability, architecture, and integration with classical infrastructure. These are deeply interdisciplinary problems, spanning physics, engineering, materials science, and systems design.
But the technical challenge is only part of the story. Realising the full potential of quantum will require systemic coordination - across academia, industry, investment, and government. Right now, timelines and incentives are misaligned. Startups are underfunded and face talent bottlenecks. Academia is stretched under outdated funding models and policy constraints. Industry engagement is slow. The ecosystem lacks cohesion. Quantum isn’t just a scientific challenge - it’s a systems-level one.
And beneath that lies something deeper. The technologies we build today reflect the structures we’ve inherited: legacy infrastructure, legacy academia, and legacy culture. Too many of the companies shaping this future still operate within privileged, exclusionary systems. If we want quantum to serve society, we need to reimagine who builds it - and for whom. We have a once-in-a-generation opportunity to do things differently. The question is whether we’re brave enough to take it.
In your view, what are the issues women working in quantum science and technology are facing in terms of diversity and inclusion in academia and/or industry? What has been your experience?
Women in quantum, and tech more broadly, still face a landscape shaped by exclusion, often in subtle but pervasive ways. From underrepresentation in funding and leadership to the emotional labour of constantly needing to prove our competence, the barriers are both structural and cultural. I’ve led organisations, raised significant capital, and helped shape national strategy - yet I’ve still been underestimated, underpaid or made to feel out of place because of my gender.
There’s also a deeper emotional toll that comes from navigating systems not built for us. I’ve spoken openly about moments I didn’t feel safe, seen, or supported. And I’ve also seen what becomes possible when women are empowered to lead in ways that feel authentic - when we stop adapting to broken systems and start redesigning them.
I’ve had the privilege of working with and witnessing incredible women across the quantum ecosystem who are doing just that. This isn’t just about fairness. Diverse teams ask better questions, build stronger technologies, and drive more human-centred innovation. The future of quantum - like the future of science - depends on who gets to shape it. And I’m hopeful, because I’ve seen what can happen when we do.
How do you see your future career moving forward? What influences this view?
I’m building toward a future that combines deep technology, systems change, and public purpose. After years at the forefront of quantum, I’m now focused on creating the conditions for transformative innovation - not just within the lab, but across how we fund, govern, and scale breakthrough science over the next 20 years. I see myself continuing to shape organisations and ecosystems that challenge the status quo, whether by founding new ventures, advising governments, or nurturing the next generation of talent.
What influences this view is a sense of responsibility - born from experience, and underpinned by hope. I’ve seen how exclusionary systems can hold back not only people, but progress. I believe the next frontier of science will demand new leadership models: principled, emotionally intelligent, and inclusive. My career will always follow that thread - building the future from a place of courage, compassion, and belief in what’s possible when we do things differently.
How does your gender intersect with your identity as a scientist?
Being a woman in science, and especially in leadership, means constantly navigating tension. There’s the external tension: being taken seriously in rooms where the norms were built by and for men. And the internal tension: wanting to lead in a way that feels authentic to who I am, even when that way is misread.
The roles I embody often carry traditional connotations of power – and I have chosen to lead not through dominance, but through empathy, emotional intelligence, and love-based leadership. When I talk about love in this context, I don’t mean something soft or sentimental – I mean leading from a place of care, courage, and truth rather than fear. At OQC, we operationalised this in our culture: love meant camaraderie, radical candour, and holding each other accountable because we cared enough to do the hard things. Steve Farber puts it well: “Love is just damn good business.” That hasn’t always been easy. Be strong and you’re called aggressive; be soft and you’re seen as weak or incapable. I’ve delivered tough decisions only to be told I was either too cold or too soft. Many people’s only reference point to a woman in a senior position is their mother - or headmistress - and that’s a projection you get a lot. In short, the double bind and bias are real, and exhausting.
And then there’s the deeper personal complexity I have grappled with of womanhood: I am a feminist who leads with love. I embrace my femininity and still want to be held, to feel safe, to be in partnership. I want one day to be a mother, and I know how much harder that is in the world I operate in. I’ve been through divorce, heartbreak, leadership challenges, and moments that shattered my world and my confidence.
But I’ve rebuilt. And in that rebuilding, I’ve asked myself again and again what it means to be a woman, a feminist - and feminine - not just in science, but in life.
I don’t want to be defined by my gender - but I also won’t pretend it hasn’t shaped me. It’s made me more intentional, more aware, and more committed to designing spaces where others can show up fully too.
Because in the end, I don’t believe that power, science, and femininity are at odds. I believe that love is just damn good business. And I believe that if we’re brave enough to lead with our whole selves, science and society are all the better for it.
Have you engaged in changing systems to remove gender bias and act as gatekeepers? This could be in any type of role: Improving review systems, hiring, student admissions, etc …
Yes, and I believe it’s one of the most important responsibilities of leadership. I’ve tried my best to act within the systems I’ve operated in, recognising that no one gets it perfect. During my time building OQC from the ground up, we focused on building inclusive infrastructure. We embedded inclusive hiring practices, ran sponsorship competitions for women in quantum, and established a diversity and inclusion working group. We created an internal values-led culture grounded in psychological safety and emotional intelligence.
Personally, I mentored early-career women in quantum and consistently centred inclusion in how I led - in decision-making and team dynamics. We led with values, and love wasn’t just spoken about, but operationalised - meaning we built structures of mutual care, candour, and accountability, not fear. I was proud to help create a culture where people felt safe to show up as themselves.
I’ve also spoken up publicly and behind closed doors about the need for reform. From boardrooms to advisory panels, I’ve done my best to use my voice to challenge defaults. I recognise the cost of that, too - being ‘the voice’ comes with trade-offs. But I believe it’s worth it.
And while I’m proud of what we built, I know how fragile this kind of progress can be. Leadership changes, priorities shift - and without continued commitment, it’s easy to slip backwards. It’s a stark reminder that inclusion is not a checkbox, but a continual practice. For quantum to reach its full potential, we must keep returning to the question: who are we building this for, and who gets to build it?
Do you have a mentor? If so, what was the best advice they gave you?
I’ve had the privilege of working with a constellation of mentors and coaches over the years. It’s something I’ve actively sought out - because as a founder and leader, you have to keep growing faster than the company you’re building. I don’t have time to learn wisdom through time alone. These people have helped me stretch, reflect, recover, and reimagine.
Their roles have varied: some have helped me sharpen strategy, others have helped me reconnect to my intuition. They’ve offered challenge and comfort, data and grace. And they’ve given me more wisdom than I could ever capture on a single page.
And yet, I’ve occasionally faced criticism for it. The idea that I’ve leaned on mentors and coaches has, at times, been held against me - as though it implies I’m not capable of making decisions myself. It’s a criticism I find deeply gendered. A man in my position would be praised for seeking counsel. For me, it’s occasionally been framed as weakness.
Many of my mentors have been men - brilliant and generous people who’ve sat in proximity to my experience as a woman in leadership and been humbled by what they’ve witnessed. They’ve told me they’ve learned as much from the experience as I have. And to me, that’s what mentorship should be: mutual growth, not hierarchy.
As for the best advice? It’s hard to choose. But one I return to often is this: “Get out of your head and into your body.” As a scientist and a leader, it’s easy to overthink, analyse, and rationalise every decision. But some of the most important calls I’ve made came not from logic alone, but from intuition, presence, and embodiment. That reminder has helped me lead with clarity, connect with people more deeply, and stay grounded when the pressure is highest. And, perhaps unsurprisingly given how much I harp on about it, another mantra I live by is: “Lead from love, not fear.” As my friend Steve Farber says, “Love is just damn good business.” And I’ve seen, time and again, love really does win.
This interview was conducted by the editors of Communications Physics.
